indication: New onset heart failure, chest discomfort, evaluate for coronary artery disease and myocardial ischemia
technique: Rest and pharmacologic stress myocardial perfusion SPECT imaging was performed using Tc-99m sestamibi. Regadenoson was administered for pharmacologic stress due to inability to achieve adequate exercise capacity. Gated SPECT images were obtained at rest.

protocol.stressResponse: Heart rate increased from 72 to 98 bpm. Blood pressure 152/84 at baseline, 144/76 at peak stress.
protocol.symptoms: Patient reported mild dyspnea during stress, no chest pain. Resolved within 3 minutes.
protocol.ecg: Baseline: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response. Stress: Non-specific ST-T wave changes, not meeting criteria for ischemia.

perfusion.stress: Moderate fixed perfusion defect involving the inferior wall (approximately 10% of the myocardium). Mild reversible perfusion defect in the inferolateral wall and lateral wall.
perfusion.rest: Moderate fixed perfusion defect in the inferior wall persists at rest. The inferolateral and lateral wall defects show partial fill-in at rest, consistent with ischemia.

function.wallMotion: Hypokinesis of the inferior wall. Mild hypokinesis of the inferolateral wall.

impression: Moderate fixed perfusion defect in the inferior wall (approximately 10% of the myocardium), consistent with prior infarction in the RCA territory.
Mild reversible perfusion defect in the inferolateral and lateral walls, suggesting inducible ischemia in the RCA/LCx watershed territory.
Moderately reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 36%) with regional wall motion abnormalities.
Findings suggest ischemic cardiomyopathy with a combination of fixed (scar) and reversible (ischemic) defects.
Total perfusion defect is moderate in severity.
recommendations: Coronary angiography recommended to define coronary anatomy
Initiate guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Risk factor modification including diabetes and hypertension management
comparison: No prior myocardial perfusion study available for comparison.
